attributes.stem:
A 74-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a three-hour history of vertigo and ringing in her ears, which she noticed upon waking. This is accompanied by aural fullness and hearing loss in the right ear. She has no headache, nausea, vomiting, or recent illnesses. She denies photophobia and phonophobia. The vertigo is not precipitated by any change in movements. Her medical history includes four lacunar ischaemic strokes and mitral regurgitation, with subsequent mechanical valve replacement. Her regular medications include warfarin, lisinopril, denosumab and glyceryl trinitrate spray. She has no history of migraine.

On examination, she appears well. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Cranial nerve examination is remarkable for slurred speech, pre-existing in the context of previous strokes. A HINTS+ exam shows a positive head-impulse test with corrective saccade. Nystagmus is horizontal and unilateral. Rinne's test shows air conduction greater than bone bilaterally; Weber's lateralises to her left ear. There is no skew deviation. The ear and ENT examination is unremarkable. Her peripheral neurological examination is normal, and no new weakness or sensory changes are identified. Her baseline laboratory investigations are all normal, as is a CT head.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a three-hour history of constant vertigo, tinnitus, aural fullness, and hearing loss in the right ear. This constellation of symptoms is highly characteristic of Meniere's disease. Meniere's disease typically presents with episodes of vertigo lasting from 20 minutes to several hours, which aligns with this patient's presentation.

The HINTS+ exam is crucial when assessing vertigo as it helps to clinically distinguish between central and peripheral causes. In this case, the HINTS+ exam suggests a peripheral cause:

  • Head Impulse: Abnormal with corrective saccades (typical of peripheral causes)
  • Nystagmus: Horizontal and unilateral (typical of peripheral causes)
  • Test of Skew: No skew deviation (typical of peripheral causes)
  • + New hearing loss: Present (can occur in both central and peripheral causes)

The hearing tests further support Meniere's disease:

  • Rinne's test shows air conduction > bone conduction bilaterally, which is normal
  • Weber's test lateralises to the left (unaffected) ear, indicating sensorineural hearing loss in the right ear

These findings are consistent with the fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss characteristic of Meniere's disease.

Vestibular migraine is less likely due to the absence of headache, lack of migraine history, and the presence of hearing loss and aural fullness, which are not typical features of vestibular migraine.

Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) is unlikely because the vertigo is constant and not triggered by positional changes. BPPV typically causes brief episodes of vertigo lasting seconds to minutes.

Vestibular neuritis is a consideration but less likely due to the presence of hearing loss and tinnitus, which are not typical features. Additionally, vestibular neuritis is often preceded by a viral illness, which is not present in this case.

Labyrinthitis can present similarly to Meniere's disease with vertigo and hearing loss. However, it typically follows a viral illness and is usually associated with more severe nausea and vomiting, which are absent in this case.

In summary, the combination of prolonged vertigo, tinnitus, aural fullness, and unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, along with the HINTS+ exam suggesting a peripheral cause, makes Meniere's disease the most likely diagnosis in this scenario.
learningPoints: Meniere's disease is characterized by episodes of vertigo, fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness.
The HINTS+ exam is crucial for differentiating between central and peripheral causes of vertigo.
Meniere's disease typically presents with prolonged episodes of vertigo lasting 20 minutes to several hours.
references: Nakashima T, Pyykkö I, Arroll MA, Casselbrant ML, Foster CA, Manzoor NF, et al. Meniere's disease. Nature Reviews Disease Primers. 2016;2(1):16028. Available at: https://www.nature.com/articles/nrdp201628
Wahid NWB, Hogan CJ, Attia M. Weber test. In: National Centre for Biotechnology Information [online]. 2023 [cited 2023 Jul 26]. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526135/

Simpson MD, Kalivoda E. Take a hint on central vertigo in the emergency department. In: Emergency Medicine Resident’s Association [online]. 2019 [cited 2023 Jul 26]. Available at: https://www.emra.org/emresident/article/hints-exam
Kong EL, Fowler JB. Rinne Test. In: Statpearls [online]. 2023 [cited 2023 Jul 26]. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28613725/
